section.title: The Cities of Refuge
section.sectionRationale: Among the allotments, God commands a provision of mercy: six cities of refuge, set apart so that a person who kills someone accidentally can flee there and be safe from the avenger of blood until a fair trial, and remain until the death of the high priest. It is one brief but profound movement, the cities of refuge, weaving justice and mercy together and pointing with unusual clarity to Christ, the refuge of sinners and the High Priest whose death sets the guilty free.
section.thesis: God builds mercy into the very structure of the land, providing refuge for the one who kills unintentionally so that justice is done without vengeance, and tying the manslayer's freedom to the death of the high priest, a provision that shadows Christ, our refuge from judgment and the High Priest whose death releases His people.
section.frameNote: Two beats: God's command to appoint the cities of refuge and the law governing them, verses 1 to 6; and the six cities named on both sides of the Jordan, open to Israelite and foreigner alike, verses 7 to 9.

units.label: The command and law of refuge
units.note: until the death of the high priest

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Joshua,
“Tell the Israelites to designate the cities of refuge, as I instructed you through Moses,
so that anyone who kills another unintentionally or accidentally may flee there. These will be your {{miqlat|refuge}} from the {{goel|avenger}} of blood.
When someone flees to one of these cities, stands at the entrance of the city gate, and states his case before its elders, they are to bring him into the city and give him a place to live among them.
Now if the avenger of blood pursues him, they must not surrender the manslayer into his hand, because that man killed his neighbor accidentally without prior malice.

He is to stay in that city until he stands trial before the assembly and until the death of the {{kohen|high priest}} serving at that time. Then the manslayer may return to his own home in the city from which he fled.”

units.label: The six cities named
units.note: cities for Israelites and foreigners alike

verses.text: So they set apart Kedesh in Galilee in the hill country of Naphtali, Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim, and Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron) in the hill country of Judah.

And beyond the Jordan, east of Jericho, they designated Bezer on the wilderness plateau from the tribe of Reuben, Ramoth in Gilead from the tribe of Gad, and Golan in Bashan from the tribe of Manasseh.

These are the cities appointed for all the Israelites and foreigners among them, so that anyone who kills a person unintentionally may flee there and not die by the hand of the avenger of blood prior to standing trial before the assembly.

miqlat.gloss: A place of refuge, asylum, a receiving place. The six cities of miqlat are havens where the one who has killed unintentionally is received and protected until justice is determined. They embody God's mercy woven into the land itself, a safe place from wrath for the one who flees to it, a vivid picture of finding refuge in God, Psalm 46:1.

goel.gloss: The avenger of blood, literally the redeemer, the near kinsman responsible to avenge a relative's death. The same word means kinsman-redeemer; here it names the one who seeks the manslayer's life. The cities of refuge restrain private vengeance and ensure justice through due process, holding together the seriousness of shed blood and the protection of the innocent.

kohen.gloss: The high priest, the chief mediator between God and Israel. The manslayer's release is tied to the death of the kohen gadol: only when the high priest dies may he return home free. This striking law binds the sinner's liberation to a priestly death, foreshadowing Christ, the great High Priest whose death frees His people and opens the way home.

basic.context: As the land is being settled, God commands a merciful provision: six cities of refuge, three on each side of the Jordan, spread so that anyone could reach one. Their purpose is to protect a person who kills someone accidentally, without malice, from the avenger of blood, the relative who would otherwise take vengeance. The manslayer flees to the city, states his case at the gate, and is given protection and a place to live. He must stand trial, and then must remain in the city until the death of the high priest, after which he may go home free. The cities are open to Israelites and foreigners alike. The question underneath is: how does God hold together perfect justice and real mercy, and where can the guilty find safety?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a legal provision commanded through Moses. It reflects a specific ancient system of justice and is not a template for modern law, but it reveals God's character: He takes bloodshed seriously, restrains vengeance, ensures fair trial, and provides refuge. Most strikingly, the law binds the manslayer's freedom to the death of the high priest, which the church has long seen as a pointer to Christ. We read it as a shadow of gospel refuge.
basic.originalLanguages: The cities are places of miqlat, refuge, safe havens from the goel, the avenger of blood. And the manslayer is freed only at the death of the kohen gadol, the high priest, a striking detail rich with meaning.
basic.typology: The city of refuge is a vivid type of Christ, to whom sinners flee for safety from judgment. And the release of the manslayer at the death of the high priest points directly to Christ, our great High Priest, whose death frees His people and brings them home. The refuge open to Israelite and foreigner alike foreshadows the gospel welcome of all who flee to Christ.
basic.christ: Hebrews speaks of those who have fled for refuge to lay hold of the hope set before us, echoing this very picture; Christ is the refuge to whom sinners run from the coming wrath. And the manslayer's freedom at the high priest's death points to Christ, the great High Priest, whose own death releases His people from guilt and lets them go home free. Christ is both the refuge and the priest whose death sets us free.
basic.god: God is both just and merciful. He takes the shedding of blood with utmost seriousness, yet He restrains private vengeance, demands fair trial, and provides a safe haven for the one without malice. He builds mercy into the very land, and He opens the refuge to the foreigner as well, showing that His mercy reaches beyond Israel.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 35 gives the fuller law of the cities of refuge and the death of the high priest. Deuteronomy 19 commands their appointment. Hebrews 6 speaks of fleeing for refuge to the hope in Christ. And Hebrews 9 shows the high priest's death opening the way to freedom.
basic.oneStory: In the middle of settling the land, God provides cities of refuge, weaving mercy and justice together and tying the guilty's freedom to the death of the high priest. This provision shines forward to Christ, the refuge to whom sinners flee and the High Priest whose death sets His people free, welcoming Israelite and foreigner alike into safety.

context.literary: Chapter 20 sits between the tribal allotments and the Levitical cities, both concerned with how the land serves God's purposes of worship and justice. It briefly restates a law given more fully in Numbers 35 and Deuteronomy 19, now put into effect as the land is settled. The naming of the six cities, three west and three east of the Jordan, stresses accessibility. The detail that the manslayer stays until the death of the high priest, repeated from the Mosaic law, carries theological weight that the New Testament later unfolds.
context.historical: In the ancient world, a relative of a slain person, the avenger of blood, bore the duty of exacting justice, which could lead to escalating blood feuds. The cities of refuge provided a God-ordained restraint: due process before the assembly, protection for the one who killed without intent, and a defined period of asylum. The six cities, Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron, Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan, were spread across the land and were also Levitical cities, so justice was administered in a setting of priestly instruction. Tying release to the high priest's death gave the system a fixed, non-arbitrary endpoint tied to the sacred office.
context.canonical: The cities of refuge develop the biblical theme of God as the refuge of His people, sung throughout the Psalms, God is our refuge and strength, Psalm 46:1. The binding of the manslayer's freedom to the high priest's death becomes a rich type: as the high priest's death released the manslayer, so the death of Christ, the great High Priest, releases His people from guilt, Hebrews 9:11-15. The openness of the refuge to foreigners anticipates the gospel's welcome of the nations, and Hebrews 6:18 directly echoes the imagery of fleeing for refuge to the hope set before us in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the appointment of the cities of refuge as commanded through Moses, showing God's provision of mercy and just process for the one who kills unintentionally, protecting the innocent from vengeance while upholding the seriousness of bloodshed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific legal system of refuge cities and blood avengers belongs to Israel's ancient covenant life and is not a model for modern legal systems. What endures and instructs is the revealed character of God, just and merciful, and the typological pointer to Christ as the refuge of sinners and the High Priest whose death sets His people free.

debates.question: Why was the manslayer's release tied to the death of the high priest?

views.label: A typological pointer to Christ's priestly death
views.case: The link between the high priest's death and the manslayer's freedom is unusual and pregnant with meaning; the church has long seen it as a shadow of Christ, the great High Priest, whose death releases His people from guilt and brings them home, Hebrews 9:11-15, so the law was designed to foreshadow the gospel.

views.label: A just and non-arbitrary end to the period of asylum
views.case: Practically, the death of the high priest, the highest sacred office, marked a natural, God-appointed close to an era, providing a fixed, impartial endpoint for the manslayer's confinement rather than an arbitrary term; some also see a sense in which the priest's death served representatively for the community's guilt over the shed blood.

debates.bearing: Both readings can stand together: the provision gave a just endpoint in Israel's system while, in God's design, foreshadowing the death of the true High Priest, Christ, whose death frees the guilty and opens the way home.

study.originalLanguages: Three words open the depth of this brief chapter. The six cities are places of miqlat, refuge, a receiving-place where the one who has killed without intent is taken in and kept safe; the word pictures a haven from wrath, the very idea Scripture applies to God Himself, our refuge and strength, Psalm 46:1. The danger they shelter him from is the goel, the avenger of blood, and here lies a striking overlap: goel is the same word used for the kinsman-redeemer, the near relative who buys back his family; the one who avenges shed blood and the one who redeems are named by one word, and the cities of refuge restrain vengeance so that justice, not private fury, prevails. Most remarkable of all, the manslayer stays until the death of the kohen gadol, the high priest, and only then goes home free. That single provision binds a sinner's liberation to a priestly death, and it shines straight toward Christ: our great High Priest, whose death releases His people and opens the way home, Hebrews 9:11-15. Refuge, avenger, high priest: the vocabulary of this small chapter preaches the gospel in shadow.

typology.type: The city of refuge, a haven from the avenger of blood
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the refuge to whom sinners flee from the coming wrath, laying hold of the hope set before them, Hebrews 6:18 and Romans 8:1.

typology.type: The manslayer freed at the death of the high priest
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the great High Priest, whose death releases His people from guilt and brings them home, Hebrews 9:11-15.

typology.type: Refuge open to Israelite and foreigner alike
typology.fulfillment: The gospel refuge offered to all peoples who flee to Christ, near and far, Ephesians 2:13 and Acts 2:39.

study.christ: Few provisions in the law point so clearly to Christ. The city of refuge is a haven where the guilty flee from the avenger and are kept safe, and Hebrews uses exactly this picture of believers who have fled for refuge to lay hold of the hope set before us, our sure and steadfast anchor, Jesus, Hebrews 6:18. He is the refuge to whom the sinner runs from the wrath to come, and in Him there is no condemnation, Romans 8:1. But the deepest pointer is the strange, beautiful law that the manslayer could not go home free until the high priest died. The death of the priest was the moment of release. So it is with us: we are set free and brought home not by our own effort but by the death of our great High Priest, Jesus Christ, who by His own blood entered once for all into the holy places, securing an eternal redemption, Hebrews 9:11-12. The high priest's death opened the door; Christ's death throws it wide, and the refuge stands open to Israelite and foreigner alike, to all who will flee to Him.
study.god: This chapter reveals a God who holds justice and mercy together perfectly. He takes the shedding of human blood, made in His image, with the utmost seriousness, and He will not let the guilty simply escape; there must be trial before the assembly, and the truth must be established. Yet He is merciful, refusing to abandon the one who killed without malice to the fury of the avenger, and He builds into the very map of the land a network of havens, accessible to all, so that mercy is never far away. He is impartial, opening the refuge to the foreigner as well as the Israelite, a sign that His mercy reaches beyond one people. And in the death of the high priest that frees the manslayer, He quietly writes the gospel into the law, revealing a God who will one day set the guilty free through the death of the true High Priest. Just, merciful, and gracious beyond Israel's borders, this is the God of the cities of refuge.

crossReferences.note: The fuller law of the cities of refuge, including the release of the manslayer at the death of the high priest.
Moses commands the setting apart of cities of refuge to protect the one who kills without intent.
We who have fled for refuge to lay hold of the hope set before us, echoing the imagery of the cities of refuge.
Christ, the great High Priest, by His death secures eternal redemption and frees His people, fulfilling the release at the high priest's death.
God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble, the deeper reality the cities picture.

study.oneStory: In the midst of settling the land, God provides cities of refuge, weaving mercy and justice together and tying the guilty person's freedom to the death of the high priest. This tender, striking provision points forward with unusual clarity to Christ, the refuge to whom sinners flee from wrath and the great High Priest whose death sets His people free and brings them home. And its openness to Israelite and foreigner alike foreshadows the gospel welcome extended to all who will run to Christ for safety.

quiz.q: What was the purpose of the cities of refuge?

options.text: To protect a person who killed someone unintentionally from the avenger of blood until a fair trial.
options.note: Verses 3 to 6 establish the cities as havens for the accidental manslayer pending justice.
They were havens for those who killed without malice, not prisons for deliberate murderers.

options.text: To serve as military forts on the borders.
options.note: They were places of legal refuge, not military fortifications.

options.text: To provide markets for trade among the tribes.
options.note: Their purpose was refuge and justice, not commerce.

quiz.q: Until when did the manslayer have to remain in the city of refuge?

options.text: Until he stood trial and until the death of the high priest, after which he could go home free.
options.note: Verse 6 ties his release to trial and the death of the high priest.

options.text: Until he paid a fine to the victim's family.
options.note: No fine is mentioned; his release was tied to trial and the death of the high priest.

options.text: For exactly seven years, regardless of circumstances.
options.note: The term was not a fixed seven years but until the high priest's death.

options.text: For the rest of his life, with no possibility of return.
options.note: He could return home after the high priest died; it was not a life sentence.

quiz.q: Who could flee to the cities of refuge?

options.text: Any Israelite or foreigner among them who killed a person unintentionally.
options.note: Verse 9 opens the refuge to Israelites and foreigners alike.

options.text: Only members of the priestly tribe of Levi.
options.note: The cities were Levitical, but the refuge was open to any Israelite or foreigner, not only Levites.
Refuge was for anyone who killed unintentionally, regardless of wealth.
The text explicitly includes foreigners among them.

quiz.q: How do the cities of refuge point to Christ?

options.text: Christ is the refuge sinners flee to, and the High Priest whose death sets His people free, as the manslayer was freed at the high priest's death.
options.note: Hebrews 6:18 and 9:11-15 tie the refuge and the high priest's death to Christ.

options.text: They show that God cares only about legal technicalities.
options.note: They reveal God's mercy and justice and point to Christ, not mere technicalities.

options.text: They teach that sinners must save themselves by running fast enough.
options.note: The refuge is God's provision received by fleeing to it; the deeper point is Christ our refuge, not self-salvation.

options.text: They have no connection to Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The refuge and the release at the high priest's death point clearly to Christ.

questions: The cities of refuge were a God-given place of safety from wrath. What does it mean for you to flee to Christ as your refuge from judgment?
God held together perfect justice and real mercy in this provision. How does seeing both in God's character shape the way you think about your own sin and His grace?
The manslayer was freed only when the high priest died. How does it move you that your freedom rests on the death of Christ, your great High Priest, and not on your own effort?
The refuge was open to foreigners as well as Israelites. How does the wide welcome of God's mercy encourage you, whatever your background, to run to Christ?
The manslayer had to actually flee to the city to be safe; the provision did no good unless he ran to it. Is there any way you are hesitating to run wholeheartedly to Christ for refuge?

translationNotes.note: Avenger of blood renders goel, the same Hebrew word used for the kinsman-redeemer; here it names the relative responsible to avenge a death, whom the cities of refuge restrain through due process.
The release of the manslayer at the death of the high priest, drawn from Numbers 35, carries typological weight, pointing to Christ, the High Priest whose death frees His people.
The six cities of refuge, Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron, Bezer, Ramoth, and Golan, were spread across the land and were also Levitical cities, set among priestly instruction.
